common.taglineDesc: Photograph the night sky with whatever camera you have and earn real rewards from the Astroman store.

hero.subtitle: Real-time sky positions from your location. Photograph what you find, redeem Stars for telescopes.

home.observeEarnDesc: Complete missions to unlock real rewards from the Astroman store.

steps.captureDesc: Photograph it with your phone or telescope
steps.mintDesc: Your discovery is permanently recorded on Solana

sky.title: Sky Forecast
sky.planetHint: Tap any planet for rise / transit / set details

page.title: Find it in your sky
page.subtitle: Stand outside and follow the directions below.
page.noVisible: No bright objects above the horizon right now.

tail.sun: The Sun — never observe directly without a solar filter.
tail.jupiter: Jupiter is the brightest object in that part of the sky.
tail.saturn: Saturn looks like a steady, golden-yellow point — through binoculars you'll glimpse the rings.
tail.mars: Mars has a distinct red-orange tint that sets it apart from the stars around it.
tail.venus: Venus looks like an extremely bright "star" near the horizon.
tail.mercury: Mercury looks like a bright "star" near the horizon — fast-mover, never far from the Sun.
tail.uranus: Faint and tricky — you'll need binoculars or a small telescope to actually see it.
tail.neptune: Faint — binoculars or a telescope only.

tailType.star: A bright point of light — steady to the naked eye.
tailType.starWithConstellation: The brightest point in {constellation} — steady to the naked eye.
tailType.double: Looks like a single star to the naked eye — separates in a small telescope.
tailType.doubleWithConstellation: A double in {constellation} — splits cleanly through a small telescope.
tailType.cluster: A faint smudge to the eye — binoculars resolve it into many stars.
tailType.clusterWithConstellation: A glittering knot of stars in {constellation} — binoculars are ideal.
tailType.nebula: A soft, hazy patch — best in dark skies through binoculars or a small scope.
tailType.nebulaWithConstellation: A hazy patch in {constellation} — best under dark skies.
tailType.galaxy: A pale ghost of light — needs a dark sky and patient eyes.
tailType.galaxyWithConstellation: A faint glow in {constellation} — needs a dark sky and patience.
tailType.sun: The Sun — never observe directly without a solar filter.

hero.belowSentenceA: is below the horizon. It will rise in the

fistTrick.label: Fist trick
fistTrick.body: One closed fist held at arm's length is roughly 10° of sky.

recognize.label: How to recognize
recognize.sun: The brightest object in the sky — observe only with a certified solar filter.
recognize.jupiter: Steady, golden-white. Doesn't twinkle the way stars do.
recognize.saturn: Pale yellow. Steady. Through binoculars you might glimpse the rings.
recognize.mars: Distinctly red-orange. Brighter than nearby stars.
recognize.venus: Brighter than every star. Often called the evening or morning star.
recognize.mercury: A bright point near the horizon, low in twilight.
recognize.uranus: Pale blue-green dot — needs binoculars and a star chart.
recognize.neptune: Pale blue dot — needs a telescope and patience.

recognizeType.star: A steady, sharp point of light. Pierces straight through.
recognizeType.starWithConstellation: A steady, sharp point — the brightest in {constellation}.
recognizeType.double: Looks like one star until magnified — then two appear close together.
recognizeType.doubleWithConstellation: A double in {constellation} — one star to the eye, two through a small scope.
recognizeType.cluster: A soft glow that breaks into many tiny stars in binoculars.
recognizeType.clusterWithConstellation: A soft glow in {constellation} — binoculars resolve dozens of stars.
recognizeType.nebula: A faint smear of light — soft edges, no sharp point.
recognizeType.nebulaWithConstellation: A faint hazy patch in {constellation} — soft, no twinkle.
recognizeType.galaxy: A pale, oval glow — easy to mistake for a smudge on the lens.
recognizeType.galaxyWithConstellation: A faint oval glow in {constellation} — best with averted vision.

hop.label: Star-hop
hop.body: Find {anchor} first — bright and easy. From there, hop {distance} to land on this target.

errors.locationFallback: We couldn't determine your location — showing tonight's sky from Tbilisi instead.

pointId.aria: Point and identify what you're aiming at
pointId.title: Point & Identify
pointId.subtitle: Lift your phone toward any patch of sky. We'll tell you what's there.
pointId.placeholder: Tap Enable, then aim the back of your phone at the sky.
pointId.feature1: Pan the back of your phone — labels follow the brightest body in view.
pointId.feature2: Hold steady to lock the identification. Works for planets, bright stars, and deep-sky objects.
pointId.feature3: Uses the same calibrated compass as the dome above — calibrate once, both stay accurate.
pointId.emptyHint: Pan slowly — the closest bright body will appear here.

timeline.title: Drag through the night
timeline.subtitle: See exactly when each body climbs and peaks. Snaps to peak times.
timeline.noneUp: Nothing above the horizon at this time.
timeline.chartAria: Altitude curves over the night, drag to pick a time

legend.scrub: Drag the chart to pick a time

events.title: 2026's biggest sky events
events.subtitle: Mark your calendar. Tap any card to see how to observe.

lunar_eclipse_mar3.body: The full Moon drifts through Earth's umbra and glows deep copper-red. Totality lasts 58 minutes; the full event runs about 3.5 hours from first to last contact.
lunar_eclipse_mar3.peak: Mid-totality 11:33 UTC (visible from Asia, Australia, the Pacific)
lunar_eclipse_mar3.moon: Full Moon — and the eclipse star itself
lunar_eclipse_mar3.gear: Naked eye; binoculars sharpen the colour
lunar_eclipse_mar3.tip: No filters needed. Find a clear horizon and start watching as the Moon enters the penumbra — the dimming begins subtly, then accelerates into umbral contact.

lyrids.body: A steady spring shower with a Zenithal Hourly Rate near 18, and occasional outbursts that triple it. Streaks are bright, fast, and often leave persistent trains.
lyrids.moon: Waning crescent — sets early, sky stays dark
lyrids.gear: Naked eye; reclining chair beats craning your neck
lyrids.tip: Look up once the radiant in Lyra is high (after midnight). Let your eyes adapt for 20 minutes — no phone screens, no flashlights.

mars_saturn_conj.body: Mars and Saturn pass within 0.4° — close enough to share a single low-power eyepiece field. Mars glows steady red; Saturn shows pale gold with rings barely tilted.
mars_saturn_conj.peak: Pre-dawn May 12, ~04:30 local, ~15° above the eastern horizon
mars_saturn_conj.moon: Waning crescent — minor light pollution only
mars_saturn_conj.gear: 10×50 binoculars frame both; any small scope splits Saturn's rings
mars_saturn_conj.tip: Be set up an hour before sunrise. Mars is the redder, steady dot; Saturn the steadier gold. Both ride low — an unobstructed eastern horizon makes or breaks the view.

perseids.body: The summer classic — peak Zenithal Hourly Rate of 100, with bright fireballs that often leave smoke trains lasting several seconds.
perseids.moon: New Moon — the darkest possible sky
perseids.gear: Naked eye only; cameras with 24mm+ wide lenses catch the bright ones
perseids.tip: Drive 30+ minutes out of city light. Lie back, face northeast where Perseus rises, and just watch. Counting meteors keeps you alert.

solar_eclipse_aug12.body: The path of totality crosses Greenland, Iceland, and northern Spain — about 2 minutes 18 seconds of full darkness. Across the rest of Europe and North Africa it's a deep partial.
solar_eclipse_aug12.peak: Greatest eclipse 17:47 UTC — early evening across Europe
solar_eclipse_aug12.gear: ISO-12312-2 solar filters or eclipse glasses, always
solar_eclipse_aug12.tip: Never look directly without certified protection. If you lack filters, build a pinhole projector — a single hole in card stock projects a crescent Sun onto any flat surface.

saturn_opp.body: Saturn rises as the Sun sets and shines all night, at its brightest (mag +0.4) and biggest of the year. Rings are tilted about 9° — reopening from the 2025 edge-on event.
saturn_opp.peak: Rises at sunset; transits highest around local midnight
saturn_opp.moon: Near new on Oct 4 — pristine dark sky
saturn_opp.gear: Telescope at 80×+ to split the rings; even a 60mm achromat shows them clearly
saturn_opp.tip: Wait until Saturn climbs above 30° altitude — atmospheric turbulence smears the view at low angles. Use medium magnification first; push higher only when the seeing is steady.

geminids.body: The most reliable shower of the year — slow, colour-tinted meteors at a Zenithal Hourly Rate near 120. Often produces bright multicoloured fireballs.
geminids.peak: 02:00 local time, December 14, with the radiant nearly overhead
geminids.moon: First quarter — sets by midnight, leaving prime hours dark
geminids.gear: Naked eye; serious thermal layers (this is winter)
geminids.tip: Wait for moonset around midnight, then face anywhere — the radiant in Gemini is overhead, so meteors fall in every direction.

header.title: Tonight
header.mapCaption: Center is straight up · ring is the horizon · tap a body for details
header.oneStandout: {count} bodies above the horizon. {object} is highest at {alt}° in the {compass}.
header.twoStandouts: {count} bodies up. {bright} is brightest, {high} is highest at {alt}°.
header.nextRise: Nothing bright above the horizon yet. {object} rises at {time}.
header.nothingUp: Nothing bright above the horizon right now.

ar.title: Immersive Sky
ar.permissionBody: Stellar uses your motion sensors to render the live night sky for your location.
ar.permissionInstructions: Hold your phone up and pan around — the Sun, Moon, planets, and constellations appear in the right place.

permissionItems.motion: Motion sensors (to track which way you face)

denied.title: Permission denied
denied.body: Stellar needs motion access to render the live sky.

ar.panAround: Pan around to see the planets
ar.liftPhone: Lift your phone toward the sky
ar.poorAccuracy: Compass uncertain — wave phone in a figure-8

fallbacks.noSensors: Live sky works on a phone with motion sensors. On desktop, use the horizon view instead.

picker.emptyTier: No targets in this tier right now

skymap.poorAccuracy: Compass uncertain — wave phone in a figure-8

posture.step1: Find the {name} in the sky with your eyes — that's the reference point.
posture.step2: Hold the phone vertical, screen toward you, and aim the back camera at the {name}.
posture.overhead: {name} is high overhead — tilt the phone backward until the back lens looks straight up at it.
posture.step3: Tap the green button. The compass snaps to wherever you're aimed.

chat.title: AI Companion
chat.subtitle: AI Astronomer · Online
chat.welcome: Hello! I'm ASTRA, your personal astronomer. Ask me anything about tonight's sky, your telescope, or astronomy in general.
chat.errorReply: Sorry, I couldn't reach ASTRA. Try again.

fieldBanner.label: Going off-grid?
fieldBanner.body: Get Astra Field Mode — runs on the phone, no signal needed.

field.title: Astra. On the phone. Offline.
field.lede: Astronomers travel to dark-sky sites with no cell signal — exactly where the cloud AI fails. Field Mode runs Astra entirely on your phone, so the answers keep coming when the bars don't.

online.label: Online · this app
online.title: Cloud Astra
online.body: Claude API, fast, broad. Best for planning a session at home or in the car.

offline.label: Offline · Field Mode
offline.title: On-device Astra
offline.body: Llama 3.2 1B + an astronomy RAG corpus, running on the phone. Free per call. Works in airplane mode.

llm.title: Local LLM with RAG
llm.body: @qvac/llm-llamacpp + @qvac/embed-llamacpp. Llama 3.2 1B Q4_0 over 72 chunks of Messier, constellation, and telescope reference data. Cited answers, no server.

whisper.title: Voice notes at the eyepiece
whisper.body: @qvac/transcription-whispercpp. Press, speak the observation, release. Whisper transcribes locally and saves it as a structured record — even with the phone in airplane mode.

background.title: Background sky checker
background.body: While you're under the scope, a local QVAC loop watches planet positions and surfaces alerts ('Saturn is now above 30 degrees') without a server round-trip.

cta.title: Field Mode preview
cta.body: Submitted to the Tether Frontier track of Colosseum 2026. Android only for now — iOS pending.
cta.platform: Android · physical device · ~700MB first-launch model download

field.credit: Built with Tether QVAC · on-chain proof on Solana
